The two modes: shared single lines (what this table uses) versus each cell keeping its own bordered box with spacing around it.

- **Separate boxes:** each cell is its own little bordered box with a gap around it, so between two cells you get two lines and a space, like a sheet of postage stamps.

Lean on the extension list, not the "video/" prefix.

mage/, video/, audio/, text/ (plus the one special case application/pdf) — tells you how to show it; store the raw bytes for anything regardless, and fall back to "no preview, download only" when the family isn't one you handle. Extension is just the hint you use when the browser reports no type.

That collapses your 13-and-growing list into a handful of families, and new formats work for free.

Right — two sets:

1. **Ready to use:** txt, md.
2. **Needs extraction:** everything else — but that set has a gradient of effort: light (strip tags/markup from html, rtf, svg), medium (pull text from pdf, doc, docx), and heavy (OCR images, transcribe audio/video).

So it's a clean two-way split for the design, with the second set worth ranking by how hard the extraction is.

text_tail: A footgun is a feature that's easy to use in a way that quietly hurts you later — like a loaded gun pointed at your own foot: normal, natural use eventually pulls the trigger. Here it means the design invites an ordinary action (copying a document the plain way) that silently breaks something (loses its methods) with no warning until it blows up at runtime.

se cousins, one difference: a footgun is something *you* operate that hurts *you* by your own hand — you pull the trigger doing a normal thing. A beartrap is a hidden hazard *set in your path* that snaps when you step wrong — you didn't work it, you walked into it.

So our case is a footgun (you copy the document and lose the methods) more than a beartrap (a lurking gotcha someone else laid).

The save log prints size, reported type, and the family it chose, so you can check the decisions after a drop.
